original_hypothesis.title: Public Forwarder initializer allows hostile party to become owner and register permissive request types (if deployed uninitialized)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Forwarder.initialize() is public and sets Ownable owner to msg.sender. If the Forwarder is deployed (or proxied) without atomically calling initialize, any external account can initialize it, seize ownership, and then use registerRequestType() to whitelist additional type hashes, potentially expanding what signed requests are accepted (including variants that bypass intended constraints in downstream tooling).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Forwarder is a critical trust boundary for gasless transfers and fee charging. Ownership capture enables operational sabotage and could facilitate signature replay acceptance changes. This is a common real-world failure mode when upgradeable contracts are deployed without initialization in the same transaction.
deployment_assumptions: Forwarder is deployed as an upgradeable contract (proxy or implementation) and is not initialized atomically at deployment time
Token ADMIN later sets Token.setTrustedForwarder() to this Forwarder address (or it is already trusted)
original_hypothesis.deployment_assumptions_note: If initialize is always executed atomically during deployment, this hypothesis is not exploitable.
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No Forwarder deployment/initialization script provided; only Token deploy/upgrade scripts are present.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function initialize(address) public initializer { __Ownable_init(_msgSender()); ... } and registerRequestType(...) external onlyOwner

preconditions: Forwarder contract address exists on-chain but has not been initialized yet
Attacker can call initialize() before legitimate operator does
Forwarder is (or becomes) the token’s trusted forwarder, or is used by integrators as an endorsed forwarder

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Forwarder ownership is established by the first initialize() caller; no constructor locks initialization.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: If Token ADMIN trusts this Forwarder, it becomes a privileged fee-charging caller into Token.payGaslessBasefee().
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From deployment until initialize is called (or if a proxy is deployed and left uninitialized).

operational_questions: Is Forwarder deployed via proxy or as a standalone instance, and is initialize executed atomically at deployment?
Is the Forwarder address currently set as trustedForwarder in the token?
Are there multiple Forwarders in use, and are any uninitialized?
validation_requirements: Check on-chain Forwarder owner() and whether initialize has been called
Review deployment pipeline/runbooks for Forwarder initialization
Confirm Token._trustedForwarder value and any rotation history

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor for new Forwarder deployments (or locate an uninitialized Forwarder).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Register additional request types to expand accepted requestTypeHash space and confuse/verifier tooling.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Exploit downstream meta-tx handling differences or disrupt relayer ecosystem (depends on how clients use typeHashes).
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Low; single on-chain transaction if uninitialized.

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Forwarder ownership takeover; ability to modify accepted request type hashes.
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Relayer infrastructure and signature verification assumptions can be broken; potential loss of user funds if a permissive type introduces replay surface in clients.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium; requires deploying a new Forwarder and rotating Token trustedForwarder (admin action).
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All gasless transfer flows relying on the captured Forwarder.

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate (understanding upgradeable init/ownership and request type hashing).
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Critical: only viable before legitimate initialization.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Forwarder owner is EOA not expected; Forwarder initialized late; unexpected RequestTypeRegistered events.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Relayers suddenly accept/deny requests due to changed typeHashes.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Owner address interacting with registerRequestType shortly after deployment.

validation_result.impact_summary: Forwarder.initialize() can be called by anyone first, granting them ownership (OwnableUpgradeable). The contract lacks a constructor with _disableInitializers(), unlike Token.sol, and no Forwarder deployment script exists to ensure atomic initialization.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The Forwarder contract inherits OwnableUpgradeable and uses the Initializable pattern. Its initialize() function is public with the `initializer` modifier and calls `__Ownable_init(_msgSender())`, which sets the caller as owner. Crucially, unlike Token.sol which has `constructor() initializer {}` to lock the implementation contract, Forwarder.sol has NO constructor at all. This means:

1. If deployed behind a proxy, the implementation contract itself remains uninitialized and anyone can call initialize on it directly.
2. If deployed as a standalone contract, there is a race window between deployment and initialization.
3. No Forwarder deployment script exists in the provided codebase, making the deployment procedure unverifiable.

Once an attacker captures ownership, they can call registerRequestType() to add arbitrary request type hashes to the Forwarder's typeHashes mapping. While the direct financial impact of adding type hashes is limited (the domainSeparator issue is a separate vulnerability), ownership capture of the Forwarder is a privilege escalation. If this Forwarder is later set as the Token's trustedForwarder, the attacker-controlled Forwarder could be used to manipulate meta-transaction flows.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Token.sol has a constructor guard at approximately line 55: `constructor() initializer {}`. Forwarder.sol has NO constructor whatsoever. The Forwarder's initialize function at approximately line 49 is `public initializer` and sets ownership via `__Ownable_init(_msgSender())`. The registerRequestType function at approximately line 128 is `external onlyOwner`, meaning only the initialized owner can register new type hashes. No deployment script for Forwarder exists - only deployToken.js and upgradeToken.js are provided.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Public initializer with no constructor guard (_disableInitializers). First caller becomes owner. No access control beyond the one-time initializer modifier.
Token.sol properly includes a constructor with initializer modifier to lock the implementation contract. Forwarder.sol is missing this entirely, demonstrating the defect by comparison.
Protected by onlyOwner - if an attacker captures ownership via initialize(), they gain access to register arbitrary request types.

calling_sequence: 1. Forwarder is deployed (either standalone or as proxy implementation) without atomic initialization
3. initializer modifier passes (contract not yet initialized)
5. Request type is registered, _eurf and _eurfAddress are set to attacker's choice
6. Attacker now owns the Forwarder and can call registerRequestType()
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Forwarder has no constructor calling _disableInitializers(). The initialize() function at Forwarder.sol:49 is public with only the initializer modifier as protection. If the deployment does not atomically call initialize in the same transaction as contract creation (or proxy creation), there is a window where any external party can call initialize and become the owner via __Ownable_init(_msgSender()) at Forwarder.sol:50.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Forwarder is deployed as an upgradeable contract (proxy or implementation) and is not initialized atomically at deployment time
Token ADMIN later sets Token.setTrustedForwarder() to this Forwarder address (or it is already trusted)
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Only deployToken.js and upgradeToken.js are provided. deployToken.js uses `hre.upgrades.deployProxy(Token, [], { initializer: 'initialize', kind: 'uups' })` for atomic Token deployment. NO Forwarder deployment script exists at all. The Forwarder deployment procedure is entirely unknown.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is straightforward: (1) Observe Forwarder deployment on-chain (or find an existing uninitialized Forwarder). (2) Call initialize(anyTokenAddress) to become owner. (3) Use registerRequestType() to expand accepted type hashes. (4) If this Forwarder is set as trustedForwarder on the Token, the attacker controls a critical trust boundary. Additionally, since the attacker controls _eurfAddress during initialization, they could point it at a malicious contract, though the existing Forwarder code restricts req.to to _eurfAddress. The missing constructor guard is a concrete, verifiable code defect independent of deployment methodology - it should have _disableInitializers() like Token.sol does.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL if Forwarder is deployed without atomic initialization. Attacker gains full ownership of the Forwarder contract, can register arbitrary request type hashes, and sets the EURF token address to any value. If the Token's trustedForwarder is later set to this compromised Forwarder (or was already set), the attacker controls meta-transaction infrastructure. Even without the trustedForwarder linkage, ownership of the Forwarder is a privilege escalation that breaks the intended administrative model.
recommended_mitigations: Add a constructor to Forwarder.sol that calls _disableInitializers(): `/// @custom:oz-upgrades-unsafe-allow constructor\nconstructor() { _disableInitializers(); }`
Create a Forwarder deployment script that atomically deploys and initializes (similar to deployToken.js pattern)
If deploying as a proxy, use deployProxy with initializer option to ensure atomic initialization
Consider adding an access control check to initialize() beyond the initializer modifier (e.g., factory pattern or deployer whitelist)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Forwarder contract using simple deployment (new Forwarder()). 2. From a different account, call initialize(tokenAddress). 3. Verify that the second account is now the owner via owner(). 4. Call registerRequestType from the attacker account to confirm ownership privileges. 5. For proxy scenario: deploy Forwarder implementation, deploy proxy pointing to it without initialization, then call initialize on proxy from attacker account.
uncertainties: Exact deployment procedure for Forwarder is unknown - no script provided
Whether Forwarder is deployed behind a proxy or as a standalone contract is unclear from provided code
Whether any off-chain tooling or deployment pipeline ensures atomic initialization is unverifiable
additional_testing_needed: Check on-chain: is the Forwarder deployed behind a proxy? Is it initialized? Who is the owner?
Review any internal deployment runbooks or CI/CD pipelines for Forwarder deployment
Test proxy deployment scenario: deploy implementation, deploy proxy separately, verify initialize() is callable by anyone
